coredata.dc:title: Paper-based fluorescent sensor via aggregation induced emission fluorogen for facile and sensitive visual detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Jiafu
Li, Haiyin
Hou, Ting
Duan, Wenna
Li, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), an important reactive oxygen species (ROS), is related to the oxidative stress in organisms, and plays important roles in a variety of cellular activities as well. So it is of crucial importance to develop sensitive and accurate sensing strategies to detect H2O2 in biological systems. Herein, by taking advantage of the unique emission characteristics of aggregation induced emission (AIE) fluorogens, we proposed a non-enzymatic fluorescence platform for facile and sensitive detection of H2O2, both in solution state using fluorescence spectrometer and on paper-based sensor via visual inspection. Through the reaction between L-cysteine and H2O2, the fluorescence of TPE-M-L, an AIE fluorogen formed between maleimide-functionalized tetraphenylethene (TPE-M) and L-cysteine, is quenched, and highly sensitive non-enzymatic H2O2 assay is readily carried out. The limit of detection (LOD) of 10nM in solution state and 2.5μM on paper-based sensor were obtained for H2O2 detection, which were superior or comparable to those previously reported in literature. Moreover, by integrating glucose oxidase with the AIE fluorogen of TPE-M-L, highly sensitive and selective glucose detection was also conveniently achieved both in solution state and on paper-based sensor by the as-proposed strategy, with the LODs of 50nM in solution state and 10μM via visual observation, much better than those obtained by other fluorescence methods. The as-proposed sensing strategy was also successfully applied to assay glucose in human serum samples. Therefore, the paper-based fluorescence sensor exhibits the advantages of simple fabrication, high sensitivity and portability, and has great potential to be applied in on-site assay of H2O2 and glucose in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Paper-based fluorescent sensor
Aggregation induced emission fluorogen
Hydrogen peroxide
Glucose
Visual detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent detection of H2O2 and glucose in solution state
Paper-based visual detection of H2O2 and glucose
Detection of glucose in human serum samples
Principle of AIE-based strategy for H2O2 and glucose detection
Feasibility study of fluorescent H2O2 and glucose assay
Analytical performance of the as-proposed H2O2 assay
Analytical performance of the as-proposed glucose assay
Application of glucose sensor in human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the principle for H2O2 and glucose detection based on AIEgens.

(A) Fluorescence spectra in solution state and (B) photographs of paper-based sensor upon UV illumination (365nm) under different conditions: (a) TPE-M; (b) TPE-M + H2O2; (c) TPE-M + L-cys; (d) TPE-M + L-cys + H2O2; (e) TPE-M + L-cys + glucose; (f) TPE-M + L-cys + glucose + GOx. Conditions in (A): [TPE-M] = 20μM, [H2O2] = 1μM, [L-cys] = 20μM, [glucose] = 1μM and [GOx] = 1μg/mL; conditions in (B): [TPE-M] = 400μM, [H2O2] = 25mM, [L-cys] = 400μM, [glucose] = 25mM and [GOx] = 100μg/mL.

Fluorescence responses of the as-proposed platform in the presence of H2O2 with different concentrations in solution state: (A) fluorescent spectra; (B) fluorescence intensity at 460nm, inset: linear plot of FL intensity versus H2O2 concentration. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent measurements. (C) Photographs of the paper-based sensor under UV illumination (365nm) upon the addition of H2O2 with different concentrations: (a) 0, (b) 2.5μM, (c) 5μM, (d) 10μM, (e) 25μM, (f) 50μM, (g) 100μM, (h) 250μM, (i) 500μM, (j) 5mM, (k) 25mM. Conditions in (A): [TPE-M] = 20μM and [L-cys] = 20μM; conditions in (C): [TPE-M] = 400μM and [L-cys] = 400μM.

Fluorescence responses of the as-proposed platform in the presence of glucose with different concentrations: (A) fluorescence spectra; (B) fluorescence intensity at 460nm, inset: linear plot of FL intensity versus H2O2 concentration. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. (C) Fluorescence images of paper-based sensor upon the addition of glucose with different concentrations: (a) 0, (b) 10μM, (c) 50μM, (d) 100μM, (e) 250μM, (f) 500μM, (g) 1mM, (h) 2.5mM, (i) 5mM, (j) 25mM, (k) 50mM. Conditions in (A): [TPE-M] = 20μM, [L-cys] = 20μM and [GOx] = 1μg/mL; conditions in (C): [TPE-M] = 400μM, [L-cys] = 400μΜ and [GOx] = 100μg/mL.

(A) Comparison of fluorescence intensity at 460nm of the system in solution state and (B) photographs of the paper-based sensor under UV illumination (365nm) in the presence of glucose and interfering molecules, respectively, in which “Blank” indicates the case with neither glucose nor interfering molecules. Conditions in (A): [TPE-M] = 20μM, [L-cys] = 20μΜ and [GOx] = 1μg/mL; conditions in (B): [TPE-M] = 400μM, [L-cys] = 400μM and [GOx] = 100μg/mL.
Detection of glucose in solution-state HS samples (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Recovery (%) = [C Measured after addition / (C Measured + C Added)] × 100%.

head.ce:title: Paper-based fluorescent sensor via aggregation induced emission fluorogen for facile and sensitive visual detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), an important reactive oxygen species (ROS), is related to the oxidative stress in organisms, and plays important roles in a variety of cellular activities as well. So it is of crucial importance to develop sensitive and accurate sensing strategies to detect H2O2 in biological systems. Herein, by taking advantage of the unique emission characteristics of aggregation induced emission (AIE) fluorogens, we proposed a non-enzymatic fluorescence platform for facile and sensitive detection of H2O2, both in solution state using fluorescence spectrometer and on paper-based sensor via visual inspection. Through the reaction between L-cysteine and H2O2, the fluorescence of TPE-M-L, an AIE fluorogen formed between maleimide-functionalized tetraphenylethene (TPE-M) and L-cysteine, is quenched, and highly sensitive non-enzymatic H2O2 assay is readily carried out. The limit of detection (LOD) of 10nM in solution state and 2.5μM on paper-based sensor were obtained for H2O2 detection, which were superior or comparable to those previously reported in literature. Moreover, by integrating glucose oxidase with the AIE fluorogen of TPE-M-L, highly sensitive and selective glucose detection was also conveniently achieved both in solution state and on paper-based sensor by the as-proposed strategy, with the LODs of 50nM in solution state and 10μM via visual observation, much better than those obtained by other fluorescence methods. The as-proposed sensing strategy was also successfully applied to assay glucose in human serum samples. Therefore, the paper-based fluorescence sensor exhibits the advantages of simple fabrication, high sensitivity and portability, and has great potential to be applied in on-site assay of H2O2 and glucose in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), one of the most important reactive oxygen species (ROS), is involved in many biological processes in living organisms. It has long been recognized that, as a catalytic byproduct of a variety of oxidases such as glucose oxidase, cholesterol oxidase, choline oxidase, etc., the accumulation of H2O2 is related to the oxidative stress in cells, causing aging or even cancer (Dröge and Schipper, 2007; Liu et al., 2015; Ohshima et al., 2003; Trachootham et al., 2009). However, despite its harmful effects on organisms, recent studies have also revealed that H2O2 plays important roles in various cellular activities, such as cell signaling, proliferation, differentiation, etc. (Giorgio et al., 2007; Rhee, 2006; Sundaresan et al., 1995). Therefore, it is of crucial importance to develop sensitive and accurate strategies to detect H2O2 in biological systems.

Since H2O2 acts as the substrate in many enzymatic reactions, enzyme-based biosensing strategies are conveniently adopted for H2O2 detection (Chen et al., 2008; Fan et al., 2015, 2016; Radhakrishnan and Kim, 2015; Zhang et al., 2008). However, the development of enzyme-based methods were often hindered by such limitations as high cost, tedious immobilization and/or stabilization procedures, and being easily affected by environmental conditions to induce instability or poor reproducibility (Chen et al., 2014b). Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop non-enzymatic H2O2 assays, which have thus received ever increasing attention. Up to now, all sorts of analytical techniques, including fluorescence, colorimetry, electrochemistry, photoelectrochemistry and chemiluminescence, have been adopted for enzyme-free H2O2 detection (Chen et al., 2014a, 2014b; Hou et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2015, 2017a; Luo et al., 2015; Narayanaswamy et al., 2016; Xi et al., 2015). Among them, fluorescence strategies have received special attention due to their outstanding feature of high sensitivity. All sorts of fluorescent signaling probes, such as organic fluorophores (either alone or incorporated with nanomaterials or nucleic acids) (Narayanaswamy et al., 2016; Xiao et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2016), metal complexes (Hitomi et al., 2013), metal nanoclusters (Guan et al., 2016; Jia et al., 2014), and carbon-based nanomaterials (Jia et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2015) have been thoroughly investigated and successfully adopted in H2O2 sensing in solution state, which demonstrated good fluorescence emission properties. However, such fluorescent signaling probes also suffered from the aggregation-caused quenching effect and resulted in reduced fluorescence at high concentration or in aggregated state, thus limiting the potential of their application in fabricating portable solid-state fluorescence sensors (Mei et al., 2015).

Aggregation induced emission (AIE) is a unique photophysical phenomenon associated with the aggregation of chromophores (Mei et al., 2015). Since the concept of AIE was first introduced by Tang and co-workers (Luo et al., 2001), AIE fluorogens (AIEgens) have undergone extensive development and been adopted to construct all sorts of chemosensors and biosensors for the assay of a variety of analytes (Chen et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2016; Kwok et al., 2015; Li et al., 2016; Liang et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2016, 2017b; Mei et al., 2015; Niu et al., 2017; Song et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2014). Due to the fact that AIEgens are weakly emissive or nonemissive in dilute solutions, but emit efficiently once aggregated, several AIE-based solid-state luminescent probes have been conveniently fabricated, which possessed the features of portability, easy fabrication, simplicity and low cost (Chang et al., 2016; Cheng et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2016; Song et al., 2016). For example, our group constructed a paper-based fluorescent sensor based on the AIE effect for the rapid naked-eye detection of both acetylcholinesterase activity and organophosphorus pesticides (Chang et al., 2016). Cheng et al. used thin layer chromatography (TLC) plate as the substrate to construct fluorogenic probe for naked-eye detection of toxic and hazardous hydrazine (Cheng et al., 2017). Tang and co-workers constructed an AIE-active bioprobe mediated by hydrogen-bonding interaction for H2O2 and glucose detection both in solution and in solid state (Song et al., 2016). Although this approach exhibited good performance, there is still room to further improve the sensitivity of AIE-based approaches for H2O2 detection.

By taking advantage of the unique emission characteristics of AIEgens in solid state, herein, we constructed a paper-based non-enzymatic fluorescence sensor for facile and sensitive detection of H2O2. The nonemissive TPE-M, i.e. maleimide-functionalized tetraphenylethene (TPE), reacted with L-cysteine (L-cys) to form TPE-M-L, which emitted strongly in aggregated state. However, in the presence of H2O2, L-cys was oxidized by H2O2 to form cystine, resulting in less TPE-M-L molecules and the subsequent decline of the fluorescence intensity. Since the decrease of the fluorescence intensity was dependent on H2O2 concentration, quantitative determination of H2O2 was readily realized. Moreover, due to the fact that the oxidation of D-glucose catalyzed by glucose oxidase (GOx) produces H2O2, glucose detection both in solution state and in solid state can also be conveniently achieved by the as-proposed strategy. Therefore, this paper-based fluorescence sensor, which exhibits the advantages of simple fabrication, high sensitivity and portability, has great potential to be applied in the on-site assay of H2O2 and glucose in real samples.

GOx and L-cys were purchased from Sangon Biotech Company, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). H2O2, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, hydrochloric acid (HCl), and acetonitrile were purchased from Dingguo Biotechnology Company Ltd. (Beijing, China). Maleic anhydride, 1-bromo-1,2,2-triphenylethylene, tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB), 4-aminophenylphenyboronic acid, tetrakis(triphenylphosphine) palladium [Pd(PPh3)4], acetic anhydride, tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone, chloroform and dichloromethane were purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). β-D-Glucose was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Japan). Maltose, sucrose, lactose and mannose were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and tryptophan, lysine and L-threonine were from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification or treatment. Whatman chromatography paper was from GE Healthcare UK Limited (Buckinghamshire, UK). Ultrapure water (resistivity of 18.2MΩcm @ 25°C) obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore Corp., Bedford, MA, USA) was used throughout the experiments.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a xenon lamp as the excitation source and a personal computer as the data processing unit. The slits for excitation and emission were set to 5.0nm and 10.0nm respectively, and the excitation voltage was 700V. The excitation wavelength was set to 365nm, and the emission spectra from 430nm to 650nm were collected. The fluorescence intensity at 460nm was used to evaluate the performance of the proposed H2O2 and glucose fluorescence sensor. A portable UV lamp was used as the light source for the paper-based naked-eye detection of H2O2 and glucose.

TPE-M was prepared according to the procedures reported in our previous work (Chang et al., 2016), with its synthetic route shown in Fig. S1 (in Supporting information). Briefly, TPE-NH2 was first synthesized through the reaction between 1-bromo-1,2,2-triphenylethylene and 4-aminophenylphenyboronic acid in THF in the presence of Pd(PPh3)4 and TBAB under basic condition. Next, 1.74g of the as-synthesized TPE-NH2 was dissolved in 30mL chloroform and 10mL acetone, and 0.735g of maleic anhydride was then added. After being allowed to react at room temperature for 24h, the mixture was filtered, and the obtained precipitate was dissolved in 35mL acetic anhydride containing 0.328g of NaOAc. The reaction mixture was then heated up to 110°C and kept at this temperature under stirring for 24h to complete the reaction, and then quenched with ice-cold water to stop the reaction. Next, the resulting solution was extracted three times using dichloromethane. Upon the removal of dichloromethane under reduced pressure, the crude product was obtained, which was subsequently purified by silica gel column chromatography using n-hexane/dichloromethane (volume ratio of 1:2) as the eluent. Eventually, the fine product of TPE-M as white power was obtained, with a yield of 65%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of H2O2 and glucose in solution state
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical assay of H2O2, 15μL of H2O2 solution with different concentrations was added to 15μL of L-cys aqueous solution (400μM) and incubated at 37°C for 30min. Next, 15μL of TPE-M (400μM) was added to the above mixture solution. After incubation at 37°C for another 10min, phosphate buffer (PB, 20mM, pH 7.0) was added to bring the final volume to 300μL. Then, the fluorescence spectra were recorded. Moreover, in a typical assay of glucose, 15μL of GOx (20μg/mL) was mixed with 15μL of glucose solution with certain concentration and incubated at 37°C for 30min, and then the solution was heated up to 90°C for 5min to terminate the reaction. Next, 15μL of L-cys aqueous solution (400μM) was added to the resulting solution and incubated at 37°C for 30min. Then 15μL of TPE-M acetonitrile solution (400μM) was added to the above mixture solution. After incubation at 37°C for another 10min, PB (20mM, pH 7.0) was added to bring the final volume to 300μL. Then, the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based visual detection of H2O2 and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical visual assay of H2O2, 15μL of H2O2 solution with different concentrations was added to 15μL of L-cys aqueous solution (800μM) and incubated at 37°C for 30min. 1μL of the reaction solution was then dropped on a strip of test paper (4cm × 1cm), which was cut from a sheet of chromatography paper, pretreated by dropping 1μL of 400μM TPE-M acetonitrile solution and dried in air. The photographs of the test paper were taken under UV light illumination (365nm) in the dark. The naked-eye detection of glucose was similar to that of H2O2. 15μL of GOx solution (300μg/mL) was mixed with 15μL of glucose solution with different concentrations and incubated at 37°C for 30min. Then 15μL of L-cys aqueous solution (1.2mM) was added to the mixed solution and incubated at 37°C for another 30min. 1μL of this reaction solution was then dropped on the spot of the test paper pretreated with 1μL of 400μM TPE-M acetonitrile solution. The photographs of the test paper were then taken under UV light illumination (365nm) in the dark.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum (HS) samples were obtained from Qingdao Agriculture University Hospital. Taking into consideration of the normal glucose level in healthy human serum as well as the linear detection range of our method, for the solution-state detection, the HS samples were diluted 20,000 times using PB (pH 7.0); whereas for the paper-based visual detection, HS samples were used as it was or diluted 10 times. Next, the as-prepared HS samples were incubated at 90°C for 10min, and then the as-treated samples were detected according to the aforementioned procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of AIE-based strategy for H2O2 and glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: The principle of the as-proposed strategy for H2O2 and glucose assay is demonstrated in Fig. 1. First, tetraphenylethene (TPE), a typical AIEgen, was functionalized with maleimide group to synthesize TPE-M (Chang et al., 2016), which was nonemissive due to the exciton annihilation process associated with the n-π electronic conjugation of C˭O and C˭C groups in maleimide (Wu et al., 2015). Next, upon the introduction of L-cys, whose active thiol group (-SH) reacted with the maleimide group in TPE-M to break the C˭C bond and form a new compound TPE-M-L (Liu et al., 2010), which emitted strong fluorescence in its aggregated state and thus acted as the AIEgen. Whereas, in the presence of H2O2, L-cys was oxidized by H2O2 to form cystine, which could not react with TPE-M, thus resulting in less TPE-M-L in the system as well as the subsequent decline of the fluorescence intensity. Since the concentration of H2O2 was inversely related to the fluorescence intensity of the system, quantitative determination of H2O2 was readily realized. Similarly, as for glucose sensing, the GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose produced H2O2, which in turn oxidized L-cys to decrease the amount of TPE-M-L in the system, causing the fluorescence intensity to decline. Whereas, in the absence of glucose, no H2O2 was produced, and the fluorescence intensity of TPE-M-L remained intact. Therefore, via the fluorescence intensity change induced by the reaction between H2O2 and L-cys, convenient and sensitive assays of both H2O2 and glucose were conveniently realized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility study of fluorescent H2O2 and glucose assay
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the as-proposed strategy for the detection of H2O2 and glucose in solution state was studied and the results are shown in Fig. 2A. Strong fluorescence emission was observed in the presence of TPE-M and L-cys (curve c), indicating the formation of AIEgens; however, with H2O2 being added to the system containing TPE-M and L-cys, the fluorescence intensity decreased dramatically (curve d), which was directly related to the presence of H2O2 and could be adopted for H2O2 detection. Similarly, as compared to the strong fluorescence of the system containing TPE-M, L-cys and glucose (curve e), with GOx being added, obvious fluorescence quenching was observed (curve f), which corresponded well with the fact that H2O2 was formed via the glucose oxidation catalyzed by GOx, resulting in the subsequent quenching of the AIEgens through the destruction of TPE-M-L. Moreover, similar results were also observed on paper-based sensors. In the presence of TPE-M (Fig. 2B(a)) or TPE-M and H2O2 (Fig. 2B(b)), no obvious fluorescence emission was observed on the paper-based sensor upon the illumination of an UV lamp (365nm). Whereas, in the presence of both TPE-M and L-cys, strong fluorescence emission was visualized on the paper-based sensor under UV irradiation (Fig. 2B(c)), indicating the formation of AIEgens (TPE-M-L). In contrast, in the presence of TPE-M, L-cys and H2O2, the fluorescence emission became very weak again (Fig. 2B(d)), suggesting that the presence of H2O2 prevented the formation of AIEgens. Similarly, in the presence of TPE-M, L-cys and glucose, strong fluorescence emission was observed on the paper-based sensor under UV illumination (Fig. 2B(e)), indicating that glucose itself did not interfere with the formation of AIEgens. However, with GOx being added to this system, barely no fluorescence emission was observed (Fig. 2B(f)), corresponding well to the results obtained in solution state for glucose detection. Therefore, the aforementioned results clearly demonstrated the feasibility of the as-proposed fluorescence strategy for H2O2 and glucose detection both in solution state and on paper-based sensor.

In order to achieve the best performance of the as-proposed strategy, the experimental conditions were optimized. Since TPE-M reacts with L-cys to form the AIEgen, TPE-M-L, it is necessary to determine the optimal ratio between TPE-M and L-cys. Thus by fixing the concentration of TPE-M at 20μM, the fluorescence signals were measured by varying the L-cys concentration. With the L-cys concentration increased from 1 to 40μM, the fluorescence intensity initially increased and then reached a plateau after the L-cys concentration reached 20μM (Fig. S2A in Supporting information), because at this point almost all TPE-M has been consumed. Thus 20μM was chosen as the optimum L-cys concentration. Then the incubation time for the reaction between H2O2 and L-cys was optimized. Upon the reaction between H2O2 and L-cys lasting from 0 to 50min, followed by the incubation with TPE-M, the fluorescence intensity initially decreased sharply and then leveled off after 30min (Fig. S2B in Supporting information), when the reaction approached completion, and thus 30min was then chosen as the optimal reaction time. As for the detection of glucose, GOx plays an important role in the as-proposed strategy, thus the amount of GOx used in the experiment was optimized. As illustrated in Fig. S2C (in Supporting information), in the presence of 20μM TPE-M and 20μM glucose, with the concentration of GOx increased from 0.05 to 10μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply in the initial stage, then slowly leveled off as GOx concentration reached 1μg/mL. Therefore, 1μg/mL was selected as the optimal concentration for GOx. Finally, the incubation time between glucose and GOx was optimized. The fluorescence intensity showed an obvious decrease along with the incubation time increased from 0 to 30min, and then it leveled off with the incubation time further prolonged to 50min (Fig. S2D in Supporting information), indicating the completion of the reaction. Thus 30min was selected as the optimum incubation time between glucose and GOx.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the as-proposed H2O2 assay
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, the analytical performance of the as-proposed H2O2 sensor was studied. As demonstrated in Fig. 3A and B, in solution state, the fluorescence intensity decreased as the H2O2 concentration increased from 0 to 20μM. As clearly shown in the Inset of Fig. 3B, a good linear correlation was obtained between the fluorescence intensity at 460nm and the H2O2 concentration ranging from 0.01 to 1.0μM, with the correlation equation of F = −839.4 C + 1096.7 (F, fluorescence intensity, a. u.; C, H2O2 concentration, μM), and a coefficient of determination of R2 = 0.9966. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 10nM, which is superior or comparable to those previously reported in literature (Table S1 in Supporting information). Moreover, the analytical performance of the paper-based fluorescence sensor for H2O2 detection was further investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, with the H2O2 concentration being increased from 0 to 25mM, the fluorescence emitted from the sensor gradually dimmed. As compared to the case in the absence of H2O2 (image a), the decrease of the fluorescence intensity induced by 2.5μM H2O2 (image b) was readily observed by the naked eye; whereas with the H2O2 concentration elevated to 5mM, fairly weak fluorescence was observed (image j), and with the H2O2 concentration being further increased to 25mM, barely no fluorescence was visually detected (image k). Thus, for paper-based visual detection of H2O2, the detection range was determined to be 2.5μM to 5mM, with a LOD of 2.5μM. These results clearly demonstrated that highly sensitive H2O2 detection could be realized both in solution state and on paper-based fluorescence sensor by adopting the as-proposed AIE fluorogen-based sensing strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the as-proposed glucose assay
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, the capability of the as-proposed strategy for glucose detection was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4A, in solution state, as the glucose concentration increased from 0 to 50μM, a decrease of the fluorescence intensity was observed, and as demonstrated in Fig. 4B, initially the fluorescence intensity at 460nm decreased sharply, and then leveled off as the glucose concentration reached 10μM. Inset of Fig. 4B shows the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the glucose concentration ranging from 0.05 to 1.0μM, with a correlation equation of F = −626.7 C + 1134.9 (F, fluorescence intensity, a. u.; C, glucose concentration, μM), and a coefficient of determination of R2 = 0.9928. The LOD was determined to be 50nM, much better than those obtained by other fluorescence methods (Table S2 in Supporting information). In addition, the analytical performance of the paper-based fluorescence sensor for glucose assay was also studied. As shown in Fig. 4C, in the absence of glucose, strong fluorescence was visually observed on the paper-based sensor (image a), and in the presence of glucose with the concentration ranging from 10μM to 50mM, the fluorescence gradually dimmed (images b to k). The decrease of the fluorescence brightness caused by 10μM glucose (image b) was readily observed by the naked eye, thus the LOD for glucose visual detection was determined to be 10μM. As the glucose concentration increased to 5mM, the fluorescence emission could still be observed (image i), whereas in the presence of 25mM glucose almost no fluorescence emission could be detected by the naked eye (image j). Thus it is reasonable to conclude that the detection range for the paper-based visual detection of glucose is 10μM to 5mM. Therefore, using the as-proposed sensing strategy, highly sensitive glucose detection was achieved both in solution state and on the paper-based fluorescence sensor.

Subsequently, the selectivity of the as-proposed fluorescence strategy for glucose detection was also investigated by substituting glucose with four saccharides (i.e. maltose, sucrose, lactose and mannose) and three amino acids (i.e. tryptophan, lysine and L-threonine), respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 5A, in solution state, weak fluorescence intensity was obtained only in the presence of glucose, and the strong fluorescence intensity was obtained in the presence of interfering substance, comparable to that of the control experiment. Moreover, the selectivity of the as-proposed strategy was also studied on paper-based sensor, and similar results were obtained (Fig. 5B). Barely no fluorescence emission was observed in the presence of glucose (image b in Fig. 5B), but fairly strong fluorescence emission was obvious to the naked eye for all the other interfering molecules (images c to f in Fig. 5B), which was comparable to that in the control experiment (image a). Therefore, the as-proposed sensing strategy exhibited high specificity to discriminate glucose from interfering saccharides and amino acids both in solution state and on paper-based sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of glucose sensor in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the as-proposed sensing strategy for real sample application, glucose in human serum (HS) samples was detected both in solution state and on paper-based fluorescence sensor. For solution-state detection, standard addition procedures were performed by adding glucose standard solution with different concentrations to HS samples before dilution. Table 1 shows the quantitative results in solution state, and the recoveries ranging from 93.9% to 103.4% were obtained, with the relative standard deviations (RSD) all below 3.25%. Moreover, visual detection of glucose in HS samples was also carried out on paper-based fluorescence sensor. For the HS sample without dilution, fairly weak fluorescence emission was observed (Fig. S3A in Supporting information), which was comparable with that of image i in Fig. 4C of 5mM glucose. Whereas with the HS sample diluted 10 times, brighter fluorescence emission was detected by the naked eye (Fig. S3B in Supporting information), which was comparable with that of image f in Fig. 4C of 500μM glucose. Thus, the glucose concentration in HS sample was deduced to be around 5mM, corresponding well with the results obtained in solution state. Therefore, the aforementioned results confirmed the as-proposed AIE-based assay a promising candidate for the detection of glucose in HS samples.

In summary, we have developed an AIE-based fluorescence sensing strategy for the detection of H2O2 and glucose with high sensitivity and good selectivity, both in solution state using fluorescence spectrometer and on paper-based sensor via visual inspection. By taking advantage of the formation of AIEgen TPE-M-L via the reaction between TPE-M and L-cys, as well as the oxidation of L-cys by H2O2, highly sensitive non-enzymatic H2O2 assay was readily carried out. The LODs for H2O2 detection were 10nM in solution state and 2.5μM on paper-based sensor, which were superior or comparable to those previously reported in literature. Moreover, via the oxidation of D-glucose catalyzed by GOx to produce H2O2, highly sensitive glucose detection was also achieved, and the LODs of 50nM in solution state and 10μM via visual observation were obtained, much better than those obtained by other fluorescence methods. The as-proposed glucose sensing strategy also exhibited good selectivity and was successfully applied to assay glucose in human serum samples. Therefore, this facile, portable and sensitive paper-based fluorescence sensor has great potential to be applied in the on-site assay of H2O2 and glucose in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575074, 21605093 and 21675095), and the Special Foundation for Distinguished Taishan Scholar of Shandong Province (No. ts201511052).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2018.01.007.
